nuggets.headline: In Rainbows Was Mixed Analog on a Toft Desk, Ditching Digital Automation
nuggets.text: Guitarist Ed O’Brien confirmed that 'In Rainbows' was mixed entirely in the analog domain on a Toft desk, with no Pro Tools-style automation. This hands-on approach contrasts with the digital tools used during tracking, which included both Logic and Pro Tools. Thom Yorke was even pictured behind a Malcolm Toft Audio series 980 console, a point of pride for the console's designer.

source.title: Gearslutz forum discussion
source.quoteSnippet: Ed has said publicly that 'In Rainbows' was mixed analog on the Toft desk, with NO Protools-like automation.

nuggets.imageCaption: Ed O’Brien, who confirmed the band's analog mixing process for 'In Rainbows'.

nuggets.headline: The Second Guitar Arpeggio Creates a G6maj7 Chord Over the Gmaj7
nuggets.text: Listen for the fourth chord in the main progression, a Gmaj7 played by Ed O'Brien's guitar. Over that, a second guitar—played by Thom Yorke in live versions—superimposes a complementary arpeggio. This second part is an inversion of the chords from the first guitar, but its superimposition creates the richer, more complex G6maj7 harmony that defines this section of the song.

source.title: A Harmonic Analysis of Radiohead’s “Weird Fishes/Arpeggi” – Flypaper
source.quoteSnippet: On the fourth chord in the progression, Gmaj7, a second guitar superimposes another arpeggio onto the first... the overall harmony resulting from this superimposition is a G6maj7.

nuggets.imageCaption: The initial guitar arpeggio that establishes the song's harmonic foundation.

nuggets.headline: For a Different Take on Rhythmic Drive, Revisit Nirvana's 'In Utero'
nuggets.text: While sonically miles apart, the propulsive engine of "Weird Fishes" shares a philosophical root with Nirvana's rhythm section. Just as Colin Greenwood and Phil Selway create a relentless, intricate pulse, Krist Novoselic and Dave Grohl built an equally undeniable foundation of raw power. Listen to "Serve the Servants" for how Novoselic's bassline provides a melodic core against the guitar's angularity—a principle of rhythmic independence also at the heart of Radiohead's work.

source.title: Radiohead on Last.fm
source.quoteSnippet: Similar artists to Radiohead include Nirvana.

nuggets.imageCaption: Nirvana, whose rhythm section provided a melodic and powerful core.

artistSummary: Radiohead is a celebrated and confounding modern rock band known for intricate compositions that consistently push musical boundaries [CIT 1, CIT 2]. Fronted by Thom Yorke's unfaltering falsetto, their music's depth and versatility earn them continued reverence in the global music scene [CIT 13, CIT 20].
